summary: DHH argues that the Internet is democratizing knowledge and undermining the perceived exclusivity of formal education. He contends that academically trained people often develop a snobbish belief that their knowledge carries special weight simply because it was acquired through institutional channels. While not dismissing education itself, he criticizes the mindset that formal credentials should determine whose knowledge is valued. He advocates for evaluating knowledge purely on its merits, regardless of the source's credentials or status.
key_points: The Internet is eroding the mystique and gatekeeping around formally acquired knowledge
Long academic education often instills a false sense that institutionally obtained knowledge is inherently superior
Students may cling to credential-based evaluation to justify the time invested in their degrees
Knowledge should be evaluated on its merits, not on the credentials of whoever holds it
money_quotes: The magic surrounding knowledge of all kinds is fading - in large parts thanks to the Internet.
The believe that the knowledge you obtain through formal studies carries special weight is snobbish and narrow-minded.

As soon as you give in to the thinking that the rigid system of full-time teachers, lectures, and cramming is magic, you've lost the ability to take advantage of good knowledge where ever you can find it.
Maybe it's because they need to prove to themselves that the five years spend on that mighty fine diploma was worth every second of it.

If I could get one wish fulfilled for my student years, it wouldn't be to get the best grades or learn the highest number of interesting people. It would be not to lose sight of good knowledge where ever I can find it.
key_insight: Knowledge should be judged on its merits alone, not on the institutional pedigree of whoever holds it.
